comments.author: @Ahsan-y2f
comments.text: How Much Rpm To You get On Your Videos

replies.author: @TraceTheGlobe
replies.text: Glad you enjoyed the visuals! It truly is a remarkable place. 🎉

comments.author: @rickwhite4137
comments.text:
When you make a video about Norway, you should really do a better job. When talking about the Viking museum, you show the Swedish Vasa ship, which is on display in Stockholm.
Norway is far from the hardest country to live in! Millions of Americans can confirm that they truly struggle to make ends meet. This is just an example. People struggle in many countries to make a good life.
Saying that Norway is one of the hardest places to live is not true! Most people in Norway live a very good life!

replies.author: @TraceTheGlobe
replies.text: Thank you for the detailed feedback. You’re absolutely right about the Vasa ship, and we appreciate you pointing that out. We also agree that Norway offers an excellent quality of life for many people. Our goal was to highlight some of the challenges residents face, not to suggest that Norway is one of the hardest places to live. Thanks for helping us improve our content.

comments.author: @MrFalcon1980
comments.text: Nothing about Stavanger..It's probably just a fantasy city😳

replies.author: @TraceTheGlobe
replies.text: Stavanger is definitely real and beautiful, we just couldn't fit every incredible city into this one documentary!

comments.author: @Atlas-Unbound-doc
comments.text: The contrast you highlighted really stuck with me — Norway looks like paradise from the outside, but the isolation, dark winters, and cost of living make it genuinely challenging. Most travel documentaries only show the beauty. Appreciate that this one went deeper into the reality.

replies.author: @ketil4237
replies.text:
Most countries have their own unique characteristics and distinct beauty. 
Norway may seem different, but when you live there, it becomes part of your everyday surroundings. 

While Norway is far from perfect, the long, dark winters in the northern, sparsely populated areas—where the Northern Lights are the only source of illumination—can be challenging for those who love the sun. Conversely, the entire summer is one continuous stretch of sunshine.

In terms of expenses, Norway is expensive; however, the average salary is significantly higher than in many other countries. So this is mostly noticeable to visitors. 

Additionally, Norwegians enjoy benefits such as short working hours, good working conditions, generous vacation days and holidays, paid sick leave, maternity leave for both mothers and fathers, as well as free education and healthcare. Surroundings are generally safe with low crime rates
There are also many officially funded cultural activities, both indoors and outdoors, for people of all ages.

replies.author: @kilipaki87
replies.text: Lol as a Norwegian it’s not «hard», and I’m poor. It’s not hard in that we are content amidst our daily struggles, not «happy», as the stereotype says, and all of these are well known and we all do things to cope, and so its not that much of a challenge as foreigners and outsiders love to make it seems. Far from perfect, but that’s besides the point.

replies.author: @TraceTheGlobe
replies.text: That’s exactly what we aimed for — not just the stunning side of Norway, but also the realities people experience there. Thanks for noticing the balance we tried to present.

comments.author: @TullaRask
